[newspapers](https://www.google.com/search?&q=%22Light+machine+gun%22&tbs=bkt:s&tbm=bks) **·** [books](https://www.google.com/search?tbs=bks:1&q=%22Light+machine+gun%22+-wikipedia) **·** [scholar](https://scholar.google.com/scholar?q=%22Light+machine+gun%22) **·** [JSTOR](https://www.jstor.org/action/doBasicSearch?Query=%22Light+machine+gun%22&acc=on&wc=on) *(July 2018)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

(**LMG**) is a light-weight [machine gun](/wiki/Machine_gun "Machine gun") designed to be operated by a single [infantryman](/wiki/Infantry "Infantry"), with or without an assistant, as an [infantry support weapon](/wiki/Infantry_support_weapon "Infantry support weapon"). LMGs firing [cartridges](/wiki/Cartridge_(firearms) "Cartridge (firearms)") of the same [caliber](/wiki/Caliber "Caliber") as the other [riflemen](/wiki/Riflemen "Riflemen") of the same [combat unit](/wiki/Combat_unit "Combat unit") are often referred to as [squad automatic weapons](/wiki/Squad_automatic_weapon "Squad automatic weapon"). Unlike [submachine guns](/wiki/Submachine_gun "Submachine gun"), LMGs do not use pistol cartridges.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Machine gun for an individual soldier

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ae/PEO_M249_Para_ACOG.jpg/250px-PEO_M249_Para_ACOG.jpg)](/wiki/File:PEO_M249_Para_ACOG.jpg)

The Belgian [Minimi](/wiki/FN_Minimi "FN Minimi") [M249 light machine gun](/wiki/M249_light_machine_gun "M249 light machine gun"), one of the most widespread modern [5.56 mm](/wiki/5.56%C3%9745mm_NATO "5.56×45mm NATO") light machine guns amongst [NATO](/wiki/NATO "NATO") countries. This one is an M249E3 "Para" model.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fc/IWI-Negev-Zachi-Evenor-01-white.jpg/250px-IWI-Negev-Zachi-Evenor-01-white.jpg)](/wiki/File:IWI-Negev-Zachi-Evenor-01-white.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d5/Bren1.jpg/250px-Bren1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bren1.jpg)

[Bren light machine gun](/wiki/Bren_light_machine_gun "Bren light machine gun")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/75/Army_Heritage_Museum_B.A.R..jpg/250px-Army_Heritage_Museum_B.A.R..jpg)](/wiki/File:Army_Heritage_Museum_B.A.R..jpg)

[.30-06](/wiki/.30-06 ".30-06") [Browning Automatic Rifle](/wiki/Browning_Automatic_Rifle "Browning Automatic Rifle") Model 1918

A **light machine gun** (**LMG**) is a light-weight [machine gun](/wiki/Machine_gun "Machine gun") designed to be operated by a single [infantryman](/wiki/Infantry "Infantry"), with or without an assistant, as an [infantry support weapon](/wiki/Infantry_support_weapon "Infantry support weapon"). LMGs firing [cartridges](/wiki/Cartridge_(firearms) "Cartridge (firearms)") of the same [caliber](/wiki/Caliber "Caliber") as the other [riflemen](/wiki/Riflemen "Riflemen") of the same [combat unit](/wiki/Combat_unit "Combat unit") are often referred to as [squad automatic weapons](/wiki/Squad_automatic_weapon "Squad automatic weapon"). Unlike [submachine guns](/wiki/Submachine_gun "Submachine gun"), LMGs do not use pistol cartridges.

## Characteristics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Light_machine_gun&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Characteristics")]

While early light machine guns fired [full-powered rifle cartridges](/wiki/Full-powered_rifle_cartridge "Full-powered rifle cartridge"), modern light machine guns often fire smaller-caliber [rifle cartridges](/wiki/Rifle_cartridge "Rifle cartridge") than [medium machine guns](/wiki/Medium_machine_gun "Medium machine gun") – generally the same [intermediate cartridge](/wiki/Intermediate_cartridge "Intermediate cartridge") fired by a service's standard [assault rifle](/wiki/Assault_rifle "Assault rifle") – and are usually lighter and more compact. Some LMGs, such as the Russian [RPK](/wiki/RPK "RPK"), are modifications of existing designs and designed to share the same ammunition. Adaptations to the original rifle generally include a larger magazine, a heavier barrel to resist overheating, a more robust mechanism to support sustained fire and a bipod.

A light machine gun is also defined by its usage as well as its specifications: some machine guns – notably [general-purpose machine guns](/wiki/General-purpose_machine_gun "General-purpose machine gun") – may be deployed either as a light machine gun or a medium machine gun. Deployed on a tripod and used for sustained fire, it is a medium machine gun; if deployed with a [bipod](/wiki/Bipod "Bipod") with the operator in a prone position and firing short bursts, it is a light machine gun.

Light machine guns are also designed to be fired from the hip or on the move as a form of [suppressive fire](/wiki/Suppressive_fire "Suppressive fire") intended to [pin down](/wiki/Pinned_down "Pinned down") the enemy. [Marching fire](/wiki/Marching_fire "Marching fire") is a specific tactic that relies on this capability.

Lighter modern LMGs have enabled them to be issued down at the [fireteam](/wiki/Fireteam "Fireteam") level, with two or three at the section/squad level.

### Ammunition feed

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Light_machine_gun&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Ammunition feed")]

Many light machine guns (such as the [Bren gun](/wiki/Bren_light_machine_gun "Bren light machine gun") or the [M1918 Browning Automatic Rifle](/wiki/M1918_Browning_Automatic_Rifle "M1918 Browning Automatic Rifle")) were [magazine](/wiki/Magazine_(firearm) "Magazine (firearm)")-fed. Others, such as the [Hotchkiss M1922](/wiki/Hotchkiss_M1922 "Hotchkiss M1922"), could be fed either from a [belt](/wiki/Belt_(firearms) "Belt (firearms)")/[strip](/wiki/Belt_(firearms)#Feed_Strip "Belt (firearms)") or from a [box magazine](/wiki/Box_magazine "Box magazine"). Modern light machine guns are designed to fire smaller caliber rounds and, as such, tend to be belt-fed (from a [container](/wiki/Belt_(firearm)#Container_device "Belt (firearm)") attached to the gun) or from a detachable high-capacity [drum magazine](/wiki/Drum_magazine "Drum magazine"), but some, such as the [FN Minimi](/wiki/FN_Minimi "FN Minimi"), will also accept standard rifle magazine feeding as an auxiliary measure when belted ammunition has been exhausted.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Light_machine_gun&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: History")]

In 1903, French military theorists noticed that the [heavy machine guns](/wiki/Heavy_machine_gun "Heavy machine gun") of the day were of little use in infantry assaults. They determined that "the machine gun must learn to walk".[[1]](#cite_note-Chauchat-1) They researched the possibility of a light machine gun which could be carried by troops. A [marching fire](/wiki/Marching_fire "Marching fire") tactic was theorised, using incidental suppressive fire, with the advancing troops considered a deadlier threat than the un-aimed bullets, causing the enemy to fall back. The prototype guns were not approved for production, and none were in service when [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I") began.[[1]](#cite_note-Chauchat-1) The French quickly brought the prototypes to mass production to boost the firepower of advancing infantry.

By the end of [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), light machine guns were usually being issued on a scale of one per [fire team](/wiki/Section_(military_unit) "Section (military unit)") or [squad](/wiki/Squad "Squad"), and the modern infantry squad had emerged with tactics that were built around the use of the LMG to provide [suppressive fire](/wiki/Suppressive_fire "Suppressive fire").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Selected examples

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Light_machine_gun&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Selected examples")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8a/Battle_of_Changsha.jpg/250px-Battle_of_Changsha.jpg)](/wiki/File:Battle_of_Changsha.jpg)

A Chinese soldier with a [ZB vz.26](/wiki/ZB_vz.26 "ZB vz.26") light machine gun.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f3/INSAS_LMG.JPG/250px-INSAS_LMG.JPG)](/wiki/File:INSAS_LMG.JPG)

The early [INSAS](/wiki/INSAS "INSAS") LMG, a weapon of Indian origin.

A Romanian soldier instructing a [U.S. Marine](/wiki/United_States_Marine_Corps "United States Marine Corps") in clearing an [RPK](/wiki/RPK "RPK")
